serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of soy milk brewed tomato seaweed ramen, approximately 450 g, comprising noodles, broth, seaweed, and tomato components.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup, estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite: soy milk, wheat noodles (cooked), tomato broth, and seaweed components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) — component estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for soy milk brewed tomato seaweed ramen. Values estimated by weighted component analysis: cooked ramen noodles (~45% by weight, USDA SR Legacy #20113), unsweetened soy milk (~30% by weight, USDA FDC #175215), tomato-based broth (~20% by weight, USDA FDC #170457), and dried seaweed/wakame (~5% by weight, USDA FDC #168457). Macros blended proportionally. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Noodle soup GI analogous estimate: ramen-style wheat noodle soup with soy milk base; reference Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for soy milk brewed tomato seaweed ramen. GI estimated at 46 based on analogous wheat noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (boiled wheat noodles GI ~47–52; soy milk GI ~34; tomato broth negligible glycemic contribution). Weighted blend of noodle-dominant dish with soy milk base yields estimated GI ~46, classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (46 × 9.5 × 4.5) / 100 ≈ 19.7, rounded to 19.8. Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care. 2021;44(9):2098-2108.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1395 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this dish as a single 450 g serving may contribute approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~19.8 per bowl) driven by wheat noodle carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (estimated 46) of this soy milk-based ramen is favourable for blood glucose management; however, the glycemic load per full bowl (~19.8) is moderate due to the noodle portion, so individuals with type 2 diabetes should consider a half-portion or substitute with lower-carbohydrate noodles and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based soy protein, and seaweed-derived micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g) and cholesterol-free, with soy protein and seaweed providing cardioprotective phytonutrients; the primary cardiovascular concern is the sodium content, which should be moderated in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soy Milk Brewed Tomato Seaweed Ramen is a Japanese-inspired fusion noodle soup dish served in Singapore, featuring wheat ramen noodles in a slow-brewed broth made from unsweetened soy milk, ripe tomatoes, and seaweed (typically wakame or nori). The broth is simmered over an extended period to extract deep umami flavours from the plant-based ingredients, resulting in a creamy yet light soup with a tangy tomato undertone and subtle oceanic notes from the seaweed. The dish is entirely plant-based, free from dairy and animal products, making it suitable for vegan and vegetarian diners. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~62 kcal per 100 g), a reasonable protein contribution from soy milk (~3.2 g per 100 g), and is low in saturated fat. The soy milk base contributes isoflavones and calcium, while seaweed adds iodine, iron, and dietary fibre. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 46 places it in the low-GI category, making it a relatively favourable choice for blood glucose management compared to conventional pork-bone or chicken-based ramen. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, as the broth contributes significant salt; individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sodium preparation. This dish is available at Flavours by Saute at Funan Mall, Singapore, priced at SGD 13.80.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific fusion dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked ramen noodles, unsweetened soy milk, tomato broth, and seaweed. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle soup entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-documented in USDA FDC and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central; no direct HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation — plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal products or fortified ingredients confirmed; soy milk used is assumed unfortified). 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified vitamin D sources in this plant-based dish). 6. GI set to 46 (low, ≤55) based on analogous wheat noodle soup estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as (46 × 9.5 × 4.5) / 100 ≈ 19.8. 7. Sodium is the primary clinical concern at approximately 310 mg per 100 g (~1395 mg per bowl). 8. The input record classified this dish under food_group 'Beverages'; reclassified to 'Mixed' as this is a complete noodle soup dish, not a beverage. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and other micronutrients are estimated from component analysis and carry higher uncertainty than primary-source values. 10. All -1 sentinel values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from component analysis where defensible; no -1 values remain in per_100g.

Verified GI category threshold (46 ≤ 55 = low). Confirmed zero-value justifications for trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg in notes. Confirmed no -1 sentinel values remain undocumented. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 verified as passing.
